main_summary.summary: Marilyn Luper Hildreth, daughter of Oklahoma City sit-in leader Clara Luper, was interviewed in Oklahoma City on May 24, 2011, by Joe Mosnier of the University of North Carolina's Southern Oral History Program for the Civil Rights History Project. Hildreth recounts her mother's path from Hoffman, Oklahoma, to teaching at Dunjee High School in Spencer, where Clara Luper wrote a play titled Martin Luther King Jr that brought NAACP organizer Herbert L. Wright to Oklahoma City and led to the 1957 NAACP Youth Council tour through the Northern and Southern routes. Hildreth describes the August 19, 1958, Katz Drug Store sit-in at NE 14th Street, which she joined at age eight as one of the thirteen original participants, and the subsequent campaign that desegregated Oklahoma City lunch counters. She recalls residential segregation south of NE 7th Street, segregated water fountains and telephone booths, and her grandmother's work as a maid in white households to educate Clara Luper. Hildreth covers Clara Luper's 26 arrests, her instruction in Mahatma Gandhi's nonviolent methods, the 1969 Oklahoma City sanitation workers strike, Clara Luper's 1972 Oklahoma Democratic US Senate primary campaign, and the white pickup truck incident that produced the one moment Hildreth saw fear in her mother.
key_themes: Oklahoma City Katz Drug Store sit-in (August 19, 1958)
NAACP Youth Council and the 1957 Northern/Southern route tour
Clara Luper's nonviolent pedagogy and Gandhian methods
Residential and public-accommodations segregation in Oklahoma City
Clara Luper's 1972 US Senate primary campaign
main_summary.historical_significance:
Hildreth is the only living daughter-witness of Clara Luper's sit-in leadership and a participant herself in the 1958 Katz Drug Store action that predated Greensboro by sixteen months. Her testimony provides first-person corroboration of the canonical thirteen original sit-in participants, the August 19, 1958, date, the NE 14th Street Katz location, and Clara Luper's tactical strategy of pairing nonviolent training with negotiation across Oklahoma City restaurants.

The interview also preserves under-documented family-history material: Clara Luper's father Ezell Shepard denied hospital treatment because he was Black, Clara Luper's reading of the Congressional Record as a study habit, her electrician husband Calvin Luper Sr.'s difficulty managing the public pressure on the family, and the white pickup truck stalking incident as the single occasion Hildreth saw her mother face fear. These episodes do not appear in mainstream Clara Luper biographical sources and constitute primary-source additions to the Movement-family record.

chapters.title: Walk With Me Through Oklahoma City
chapters.topic: Clara Luper and the History Lesson
chapters.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Oklahoma City in May 2011, Marilyn Luper Hildreth introduces her mother Clara Luper, an educator at Dunjee High School in Spencer who wrote a play about Martin Luther King Jr. that drew an NAACP invitation, then gave her young people a history lesson on segregated Oklahoma City: stores where you could not try on a hat or shoes, restaurants that passed you a brown bag, and separate phone booths and water fountains.

chapters.title: A Taste of Freedom
chapters.summary: Hildreth recounts the NAACP trip north, where children who had never left Oklahoma sat down to drink a Coke and eat a hamburger and drank from the same water fountain as everyone else for the first time, then returned to the South to face discrimination again, carrying what she calls the dangerous taste of a little freedom.

chapters.title: Just Sit Until They Served Us
chapters.summary: At a Monday-night NAACP Youth Council meeting at the family home on NE Park Place, an eight-year-old Marilyn moved that the group go to Katz Drug Store and sit until served; she describes being spat on, kicked, and called names, the youth pressing on without fear, and the lunch counter desegregating within days as the group moved restaurant to restaurant.

chapters.part: The Woman Who Would Not Stay Down
chapters.title: You Could Knock It Down but Not Keep It Down
chapters.summary: Hildreth reflects on her mother as a woman leader when local authority was almost entirely male, a dreamer and reader who gave her children books rather than toys, faced threats to her teaching job, and was protected by a colleague who refused to fire her as the price of a principalship, and who drilled the children every morning in Gandhi's nonviolent method.

chapters.part: The Woman Who Would Not Stay Down
chapters.title: Where Did My Mom Get That Toughness
chapters.summary: She traces her mother's strength to Hoffman, Oklahoma, where Clara was near the top of a class of five with only leftover textbooks missing pages, and to a father denied hospital treatment because he was Black, a wound Hildreth believes, alongside a strong belief in God, gave her mother the resolve to keep fighting injustice.

chapters.part: The Woman Who Would Not Stay Down
chapters.title: Run and Knock That Door Down
chapters.topic: The One Time Mother Showed Fear
chapters.summary: Hildreth recalls the single time she saw her mother afraid, driving home from a freedom rally in a caravan of more than eighty cars when white men in a pickup truck began following them; Clara told the children to run for the Posey family door when she blew the horn, then drove away alone, leaving Marilyn fearing she might never see her mother again.

chapters.title: The Maids and the Garbage Workers
chapters.summary: Members of Fifth Street Baptist, the family found Clara loved, feared, and disliked among the city's Black pastors, with congregations afraid their churches would be bombed; Hildreth notes the Oklahoma City movement rested on the have-nots, the maids and garbage workers, including her own grandmother, who moved into servant quarters to educate Clara and her siblings.

chapters.title: They Threw a Chimpanzee on Me
chapters.summary: Hildreth describes a relatively low-violence Oklahoma City, which she believes consciously chose not to become a national flashpoint, a workable relationship with the police, and her mother being arrested twenty-six times without being beaten, set against a cruel incident in which someone threw a chimpanzee on her, small as she was then, while she was picketing.

chapters.title: She Read the Congressional Record
chapters.summary: Asked where her mother's political skill came from, Hildreth answers all of the above: Clara read the Congressional Record as a hobby, knew everyone in Oklahoma, and became the voice of people whose problems had become struggles; she adds that her parents divorced amid the movement, her fun-loving electrician father unable to handle the pressure.

chapters.title: A Whirlwind Effect
chapters.topic: Years of Protest and Its Legacy
chapters.summary: Hildreth remembers Saturdays of her youth spent protesting, with amusement parks closed to them and a closeness among the young participants outsiders cannot grasp; recalling the standing-room 50th-anniversary commemoration of the 1958 sit-in, she says they only wanted social change in Oklahoma City but set off a whirlwind that changed American history.

chapters.title: You Can't Out-Read Mom
chapters.summary: Hildreth recounts joining her mother's 1972 US Senate primary campaign, an unbeatable debater versed in government and education from decades of study who carried the same message to tiny all-white towns across Oklahoma and surprised people of every background; they lost the primary, but with a little more time, Hildreth believes, Clara could have made historic change.

chapters.title: So Goes One, So Goes All
chapters.topic: The Sanitation Strike and a Closing Creed
chapters.summary: Hildreth closes on the 1969 Oklahoma City sanitation workers' strike, where Clara was chosen spokesperson as an honest woman of integrity who would fight to the end for people who could not represent themselves, and on the daily creed her mother taught: to believe in the rain when it does not fall, the sun when it does not shine, and a God you have never seen.

loc_video.caption: Marilyn Luper Hildreth oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma, 2011 May 24
